cues.x: This is a deep dive into "Contagious: Why Things Catch On" by Jonah Berger.
If you make anything, a product, a service, a piece of writing, a pitch, a cause you believe in, then somewhere in the back of your mind is a quiet, stubborn wish.
You want people to talk about it.
You want it to spread.
And this book is Berger's answer to the oldest question in marketing, the one nobody had ever really cracked with hard evidence before he did.
Why do some things catch on while others, often better things, sink without a ripple?
So settle in.
Whether you are driving or on the treadmill, let me take you through what he found, because by the end of this you are going to see your own work differently.
Let me start where Berger starts, with a steak.
In 2005 a restaurant called Barclay Prime opened in Philadelphia, and the owner, a man named Howard Wein, wanted to do something that would make people talk.
So he put a cheesesteak on the menu.
Now, a cheesesteak in Philadelphia is a five-dollar street food, a thing you eat standing up with grease on your fingers.
Wein charged one hundred dollars for his.
One hundred dollars.
It had thinly sliced Kobe beef, melted Taleggio cheese, caramelized onions, shaved truffles, seared foie gras, and it came with a small bottle of chilled champagne.
And here is the thing that matters.
People could not stop talking about it.
It became a story you told at dinner parties.
Have you heard about the hundred-dollar cheesesteak?
It got written up everywhere, on television, in newspapers, and it did exactly what Wein wanted.

It put his restaurant on the map, not because he bought a lot of advertising, but because the cheesesteak itself was so remarkable that people did his marketing for him, for free, one conversation at a time.
That is the whole book in a single sandwich.
Word of mouth.
Berger opens with a number that reframes everything.
Studies suggest that word of mouth is the primary factor behind twenty to fifty percent of all purchasing decisions.
Think about that.
When you are choosing a doctor, a movie, a place to eat, a book to read, you do not usually respond to an ad.
You listen to your friend who said, you have to try this.
And there is a good reason word of mouth is so much more powerful than advertising.
Two reasons, actually.
First, it is more persuasive, because your friends have no reason to lie to you.
An ad will always tell you the product is amazing.
Your buddy will tell you the truth, that the first half was great and the ending fell apart.
So you trust it.
Second, word of mouth is targeted.
Advertising sprays a message at everyone and hopes some of it lands on someone who cares.
But when you tell a friend about a great new hiking trail, you tell the friend who hikes.
Word of mouth naturally finds the people who are actually interested.
Now here is where Berger picks a fight with the conventional wisdom, and I want you to really hear this because it changes how you should think about everything you make.
For years, marketers were obsessed with the idea of influentials.
Find the special people, they said.
Find the connectors, the mavens, the ones with huge networks, and if you can just get them to talk about your thing, it will spread like fire.
It sounds intuitive.
But Berger looked hard at the research and found that the evidence for it is thin.
Most word of mouth is generated by ordinary people, regular folks with regular-sized networks.
The average person talks about products and brands more than a dozen times a day.
It is not a special few doing the spreading.
It is all of us.
So Berger flips the question.
The obsession with the influential messenger is asking the wrong thing.
It is not about who is doing the talking.
It is about what they are talking about.
It is not about the messenger.
It is about the message.
If the message is contagious, it does not matter much who says it first, it will travel.
And that is genuinely good news for you, because you cannot control whether some celebrity picks up your product.
But you can absolutely control whether the thing you make is worth talking about.
Virality is not born.
It is made.
It is not luck, it is not random, it is not magic that strikes some lucky few.
It is engineered.
You build the contagiousness into the product or the idea itself.
So how do you do that?
This is the heart of the book.
Berger spent years studying thousands of pieces of content, products, and messages, trying to find the common threads, the shared DNA of things that catch on.
And he found six principles.
Six ingredients that, when you bake one or more of them into your thing, dramatically raise the odds it will spread.
He wraps them in an acronym, STEPPS, and I am going to walk you through all six, slowly, with the stories he tells, because the stories are where it comes alive.
The first S is Social Currency.
Here is the core idea.
We share things that make us look good.
Think about how you talk.
When you tell people about the restaurant you found, the deal you scored, the fact you happen to know, part of what you are doing, even if you would never admit it, is managing how others see you.
We are all, in a small way, curating our own image with the things we mention.
Just like the clothes we wear and the car we drive, the things we talk about are a signal, a piece of identity.
So if telling people about your product makes them look sharp, or in-the-know, or clever, or ahead of the curve, they will tell people, because in sharing it, they are polishing their own reputation.
Berger gives a wonderful example, a bar in New York City called Please Don't Tell.
To get in, you walk into a small hot dog shop in the East Village called Crif Dogs.
Inside that hot dog shop there is an old wooden phone booth, the kind with a door.
You step into the phone booth, you pick up the receiver of the antique phone, and you dial the number three.
A host answers, and if you have a reservation, a hidden door at the back of the booth swings open into a dark, intimate cocktail bar.
That is it.
A secret bar behind a phone booth inside a hot dog shop.
Now, why does a place like that succeed when it does absolutely no advertising and is by design hard to find?
Because it is a secret, and secrets are social currency.
When you find out about it and you go, you cannot wait to tell people.
Telling them makes you look like an insider, someone with access to the hidden layer of the city.
The very secrecy that should make it hard to spread is exactly what makes it spread.
People love feeling like they are on the inside.
Berger digs into a couple of mechanisms here.
One is what he calls the power of the remarkable.
Remarkable things, literally things worth making a remark about, are extraordinary, novel, surprising.
They break the pattern of the ordinary, and because they do, they give us something to say.
The hundred-dollar cheesesteak is remarkable.
And Berger points to a company called Blendtec, which sold blenders, one of the least exciting products you can imagine.
The founder, Tom Dickson, started a video series called "Will It Blend?"
In each one, he takes something absurd and drops it into the blender to see if it will pulverize.
He blended marbles.
He blended a garden rake handle.
And famously, when the new iPhone came out, he blended a brand new iPhone.
It smoked, it shattered, it turned into a fine black powder, and he held up the little jar and deadpanned, iPhone smoke, do not breathe this.
Those videos got hundreds of millions of views and Blendtec's sales rose dramatically, because who does not want to tell their friends, you have to see this guy blend an iPhone.
The blender was ordinary.
The demonstration was remarkable.
And remarkable is shareable.

The other mechanism inside social currency is what Berger calls game mechanics, the elements of a game, the points, the levels, the status markers, that make achievement feel good and, crucially, make us want to talk about it.
Think about frequent-flyer programs.
Airlines could just give you a discount.
Instead they give you a tier.
Silver, Gold, Platinum.
And people who reach that top tier will absolutely find a way to mention it, because the status is a badge, and a badge is worth talking about.
Berger talks about how these systems work by giving people something they have earned that also happens to signal how great they are.
He mentions the online retailer Rue La La, which sold designer goods but used a clever twist.
Their sales were exclusive and time-limited, they would open at a specific hour and the best items would sell out fast.
That scarcity, that sense of a limited window and an exclusive club, made membership feel special, and feeling special is something people share.
The lesson for you is this.
If you can build in a way for people to achieve something, to earn a visible marker of status or access, you give them a reason to tell the world, because telling the world reflects well on them.
Let me pause and connect this to your work.
Whatever you make, ask yourself, does talking about this make the person look good?
Does it make them feel like an insider, like they know something clever, like they are ahead of the curve?
If the honest answer is no, that is a design problem you can fix.
Build in the remarkable, build in the secret, build in the earned status.
Give people a way to look good by talking about you.
The second letter, T, stands for Triggers.
And this one is subtle but incredibly powerful.
Berger sums it up in a phrase, top of mind, tip of tongue.
A trigger is a cue in the environment that reminds people of your product or idea.
We talk about what is activated in our heads, and what is activated in our heads is driven by whatever is around us at the moment.
So the more often something in a person's daily environment reminds them of your thing, the more they will think about it, and the more they think about it, the more they talk about it.
Here is the story that makes it click.
Berger points to the Mars candy bar.
Back in 1997, the company noticed that sales of the Mars bar had ticked up, for no reason they could identify.
They had not run a new campaign.
They had not changed anything.
What had happened?
NASA's Pathfinder mission had just landed on the planet Mars, and it was all over the news.
The planet Mars was on everyone's mind, and the planet Mars is a trigger for the Mars candy bar.
People were not consciously connecting the two.
They just kept hearing the word Mars, and the candy bar got a little nudge in their memory each time, and some of them bought one.
The company did nothing, and got a sales boost from a spacecraft.
That is the raw power of a trigger.
Now compare two songs Berger discusses.
There was a famously terrible viral song by a teenager named Rebecca Black, called "Friday."
Critics savaged it.
It was widely mocked as one of the worst songs ever made.
And yet, look at the streaming data, and you see something odd.
Every single week, plays of that song spiked on Fridays.
Why?
Because Friday is a trigger.
Every week the day rolls around, everyone is thinking, it's Friday, and that thought reactivates the song.
The song had a built-in weekly reminder baked into the calendar itself, so even a song people did not particularly like kept getting resurrected, every seven days, forever.
Compare that to a brilliant, catchy song that has no link to any recurring cue.
It might get a bigger initial burst, but nothing in daily life brings it back, so it fades.
Berger's insight is that catchiness alone is not what sustains something.
A link to a frequent, recurring trigger is often more valuable than pure quality, because the trigger keeps bringing you back to mind.
Berger tells the story of Kit Kat, the chocolate bar, whose sales had gone flat.
The team ran a campaign that deliberately tied Kit Kat to coffee.
A break, a Kit Kat and a cup of coffee.
They ran it over and over until, in people's minds, coffee and Kit Kat became linked.
And coffee is a fantastic trigger, because millions of people drink it every single day, often multiple times a day.
So they took a candy bar that had no natural daily cue and welded it onto one of the most frequent habits in modern life.
Sales climbed substantially.
They did not make the Kit Kat tastier.
They gave it a trigger.
And Berger describes a study run by a word-of-mouth marketing company, BzzAgent, involving a cereal.
There was a product called Boo-Berry cereal, and a Honey Nut Cheerios type product.
The finding was that the more frequently a cue appeared in people's environments, the more they talked about the product it was tied to.
Products linked to common, everyday triggers got talked about more consistently over time than products that were, in the moment, more exciting but had no environmental hook.
The takeaway for you is powerful and a little counterintuitive.
When you design a message or a product, do not just ask, is it clever?
Ask, what in my customer's daily life will remind them of this?
Can I link my thing to a cue they encounter all the time, a moment of the day, a common object, a routine, a place?
Because a jingle or a message tied to a common stimulus that people bump into constantly will outperform something merely catchy that nothing brings back to mind.
Consider the context.
Grow the habitat of cues that trigger your idea.
The third letter, E, is Emotion.
And the rule here is beautifully simple.
When we care, we share.
When something moves us, when it stirs something inside, we feel a pull to pass it along.
So one path to getting people to spread your idea is to make them feel something.
But, and this is the crucial nuance Berger uncovers, not all emotions are equal, and this is where most people get it wrong.
Berger and his colleague Katherine Milkman did a beautiful study.

They analyzed thousands of articles from the New York Times over a period of months, specifically looking at which ones made it onto the most-emailed list, the list of articles people actively chose to share with others.
And they crunched the emotional content of each one.
What they found was fascinating.
It was not simply that positive articles or negative articles got shared more.
The dividing line was something else entirely.
It was arousal.
Physiological arousal.
The degree to which an emotion fires you up and activates your body.
High-arousal emotions drove sharing.
Awe drove sharing, that feeling of wonder when you encounter something vast and amazing, awe made articles far more likely to be emailed.
Excitement drove sharing.
Amusement, that delighted, funny feeling, drove sharing.
And on the negative side, anger drove sharing, that heated, activated fury.
Anxiety drove sharing, that anxious, worried, on-edge feeling.
All of these, whether pleasant or unpleasant, share one thing.
They rev you up.
They activate you.
They put fuel in the tank, and that energy spills over into action, into sharing.
But there was one emotion that actually made people less likely to share.
Sadness.
And that surprises people, because sadness is a strong emotion, we feel it deeply.
But sadness is a low-arousal emotion.
It is deactivating.
When you are sad, you slow down, you curl up, you go quiet, you withdraw.
Sadness saps your energy rather than charging it, and so it does not drive the outward action of sharing.
Contentment is similar, a pleasant feeling, but a calm, low-energy one, and calm does not spread.
So the lesson is not simply, make people feel good, or make people feel something.
It is, make people feel a high-arousal emotion.
Awe, excitement, amusement, anger, anxiety.
Fire them up.
Berger brings this to life with stories.

Remember Susan Boyle, the ordinary-looking Scottish woman who walked onto a talent show stage and the audience smirked, expecting nothing, and then she opened her mouth and sang, and it was breathtaking.
That clip went around the world at astonishing speed.
Why?
Awe.
The pure wonder of the gap between expectation and reality, the goosebumps of it.
Awe is one of the most powerful sharing emotions there is, and that video was awe distilled.
He talks about a video Dove made called "Evolution."
It shows an ordinary woman being transformed by makeup artists, lighting, and heavy photo editing into a flawless billboard model, and the whole thing is sped up so you watch the manipulation happen.
It ends with a line about how our perception of beauty is distorted.
It struck a nerve.
It stirred a mix of surprise and a kind of indignant awe about the unrealistic images we are all fed, and it was shared millions upon millions of times.
It made people feel, and feeling made them share.
And on the anger side, Berger tells the story of a musician named Dave Carroll.
He flew United Airlines, and while his guitar was being handled by baggage crews, he watched, through the plane window, as they threw it around, and it arrived broken.
He tried to get United to compensate him, and for months he got nowhere, bounced around, denied, ignored.
So he did what a musician does.
He wrote a song, a funny, catchy, pointed song called "United Breaks Guitars," and made a video, and posted it.
It went viral, tens of millions of views.
It tapped into everyone's simmering anger at being treated badly by big impersonal companies.
That high-arousal anger drove people to share it in droves, and it became a genuine public relations headache for the airline.
Now here is a detail I love, because it shows just how physical arousal really is.
Berger describes research showing that any source of physiological arousal, even one that has nothing to do with the content, can increase sharing.
In one study, people who had just been made to jog in place, to physically exercise and get their heart rate up, were more likely to share an article afterward than people who had been sitting still.
The exercise had nothing to do with the article.
But the arousal, the activated body, the elevated heart rate, spilled over into a greater urge to share.
That is how deep this goes.
Arousal is a state of the body, and a fired-up body wants to act, and sharing is an action.
So when you make your thing, ask, does this fire people up?
Does it make them feel awe, or excitement, or amusement, or even righteous anger?
Do not aim for lukewarm.
Aim for activated.
The fourth letter, the first of two Ps, stands for Public.
And Berger's phrase for it is, built to show, built to grow.
The core idea is about observability and social proof.
Human beings are imitators.
We look to what other people are doing to figure out what we should do.
This is the herd instinct, and it is deeply wired.
When we are uncertain, and we are uncertain a lot, we assume that if lots of people are doing something, it is probably the smart thing to do.
Berger calls this social proof, the tendency to follow the crowd.
But here is the catch.
You can only imitate what you can see.
If a behavior or a product is private, invisible, hidden, then nobody knows it is popular, and the herd instinct has nothing to feed on.
So one of the most important things you can do is make your product or idea more observable, more public.
Berger tells the story of Apple and a design decision by Steve Jobs.
On early laptops, the logo on the lid was oriented so that it faced the user when the laptop was open.
That makes sense, right?
You want the person using it to see the brand.
But Jobs made a different call.
He flipped the logo so that it faced away from the user, which means, when you open your laptop in a coffee shop and start working, the glowing Apple faces everyone else in the room.
And Apple engineers were reportedly puzzled, but the logic is pure Public.
Every person using a Mac in public becomes a little billboard, showing everyone around them that this is the cool, popular choice.
Multiply that by millions of laptops in millions of cafes, and you have an enormous, self-perpetuating advertisement, built right into the product, visible by design.
He tells the story of Movember, the campaign where men grow mustaches during the month of November to raise awareness and money for men's health.
A mustache is a brilliant piece of public behavior.
You cannot hide it.
It sits right there on your face, and for a whole month it prompts questions, why the mustache, and each question is a chance to spread the cause.
The behavior is inherently observable, and its observability is exactly what makes it grow.
Same with the Livestrong bracelets, those simple yellow rubber wristbands sold to support cancer research.
Millions were sold.
Why yellow and why a wristband?
Because it is visible.
You wear it on your wrist, out in the open, and other people see it, and they wonder about it, and they get one too.
The visibility of the bracelet is the engine of its spread.
If it had been, say, a private donation with nothing to show for it, it would never have caught fire the same way.
If something is built to show, it is built to grow.
Berger points to Hotmail in the early days of email.
Hotmail grew explosively, and part of the reason was a tiny piece of genius.
At the bottom of every single email a Hotmail user sent, the company automatically added a line, a footer, that said something like, get your free email at Hotmail.
So every message a user sent was an advertisement, seen by the recipient, who might then sign up, and then their emails advertised it too.
The product advertised itself every time it was used, publicly, automatically.
And Apple made a related choice with the earbuds that came with the iPod.
At the time, most headphones were black.
Apple made theirs white.
And suddenly, walking down the street, you could see who had an iPod, because of the distinctive white cords trailing from their ears.
The white earbuds turned every user into a visible signal of ownership, making the product's popularity impossible to miss.
But Berger also gives a crucial warning here, a cautionary tale about how making something public can backfire if you are not careful about what exactly you are making visible.
There was a well-known anti-drug campaign aimed at teenagers.
The ads ran constantly, telling kids not to use drugs, showing that lots of kids face the pressure and the presence of drugs.
And researchers found that the campaign may have actually backfired.
Because by emphasizing over and over how common drug use was, how everyone is dealing with it, the ads inadvertently sent the message that using drugs is normal, that lots of people do it.
And remember social proof, we imitate what we see others doing.
So an ad meant to say, do not do this, accidentally broadcast the signal, everyone is doing this, which is exactly the wrong social proof.
The lesson is sharp.
Making something public is powerful, but be very careful that what you are making visible is the behavior you actually want to spread, not the problem you are trying to fight.
So for your work, ask, when someone uses my product or supports my cause, can other people see it?
Is there a visible residue, a signal, a trace that lingers and tells the world, this is a thing people do?
If the answer is no, invent one.
Give people a way to show.
Because privacy is the enemy of spread, and what is built to show is built to grow.
The fifth letter, the second P, stands for Practical Value.
And the phrase Berger uses is, news you can use.
This one is the most straightforward and, in a way, the most human.
We share useful information because we want to help the people we care about.

When you find a genuinely useful tip, a way to save money, a way to do something better, a smart hack, a piece of important news, you pass it to your friends and family because it is a small act of caring.
It says, I was thinking about you, here is something that will help.
Sharing practical value strengthens our relationships and lets us feel good about being helpful.
Berger's favorite example here is wonderful.

There is a video of an older man, an eighty-six-year-old named Ken Craig, and all he does in the video is demonstrate a simple trick for shucking corn, for getting the husk and all those annoying silk strands off an ear of corn cleanly.
You microwave it for a bit, then cut the end and shake it, and the whole cob slides out clean, silk and all.
That is it.
An elderly man, a homely kitchen, a cooking tip.
And that video was shared millions of times.
It went viral.
Not because it was emotionally overwhelming, not because it was cool, but because it was genuinely, practically useful.
Everyone knows someone who hates shucking corn, and so everyone who saw it thought, I have to send this to my mom, to my brother, to my friend who cooks.
Useful travels.

Then Berger goes deep on the psychology of deals, because saving people money is a huge category of practical value, but it turns out our sense of what makes a good deal is strange and not at all rational.
He teaches you about reference points.
We do not judge value in absolute terms.
We judge it relative to some comparison, some anchor.
And this leads to one of my favorite stories in the whole book, about a bread machine.
A company, Williams-Sonoma, introduced a home bread-making machine, and at first it barely sold.
So they brought in a second bread machine, a bigger, fancier, more expensive one, and placed it right next to the original.
And here is the strange part.
The expensive one did not sell much, but sales of the original machine roughly doubled.
Why?
Because now people had a reference point.
On its own, the first machine seemed expensive, who knows what a bread machine should cost.
But next to the pricier model, it suddenly looked like the sensible, reasonable, mid-priced choice.
The comparison made it a deal.
The reference point changed everything.
And Berger teaches a specific, memorable rule about how to frame discounts, and it is genuinely useful in your own life, so listen closely.
It is called the Rule of 100.
When you want to express a discount, you can express it as a percentage off, or as a dollar amount off.
Which one feels bigger?
The answer depends on the price of the item, and the threshold is one hundred.
For products priced under one hundred dollars, percentage discounts look larger and are more persuasive.
Twenty percent off a twenty-five-dollar shirt sounds better than five dollars off, even though they are identical, because twenty is a bigger number than five.
But for products priced over one hundred dollars, the reverse is true.
Dollar discounts look larger.
Two hundred dollars off a two-thousand-dollar laptop sounds better than ten percent off, even though they are the same, because two hundred is a bigger number than ten.
So below a hundred, use percentages.
Above a hundred, use dollar amounts.
Whichever gives the bigger-sounding number.
That is a real tool you can put in your pocket today.
There is one more piece to practical value, and it is about narrowness.
It might seem like the most broadly useful information would spread the most, something helpful to everyone.
But Berger points out something counterintuitive.
Sometimes information that is useful to a narrow, specific group actually spreads better within that group, precisely because it feels so relevant.

If a tip is perfectly tailored to, say, new parents, or to runners, or to people with a specific health condition, then within that community it feels intensely, personally useful, and people share it eagerly with others they know who fit.
The narrowness makes it feel like it was made for exactly this person, and that relevance drives the sharing.
So do not always try to be useful to everyone.
Sometimes being deeply useful to a specific someone is what makes your practical value spread.
Ask of your work, does this genuinely help people?
Does it give them a tip, a saving, a shortcut, a piece of news they will want to pass along to help someone they care about?
If you can package real usefulness in a clear, remarkable way, people will do the work of spreading it, because helping others feels good.
And now we come to the sixth and final letter, the second S, which stands for Stories.
And Berger's phrase is, information travels under the guise of idle chatter.
Here is the deepest truth of the book, in a way.
People do not think in facts and figures and bullet points.
People think in stories.
Stories are how we have passed information down for tens of thousands of years, around fires, over dinner tables, in the car.
And when we share a story, we are not usually thinking, I am now transmitting information.
We are just telling a good tale.
But riding along inside that tale, like a stowaway, is information, lessons, brand names, messages.
Stories are vessels.
They are the carriers that smuggle your message from one mind to another under the cover of entertainment.
Berger reaches all the way back to ancient Greece for the perfect image, the Trojan Horse.
The Greeks could not breach the walls of Troy, so they built an enormous wooden horse, hid soldiers inside it, and left it as a gift.
The Trojans, delighted, wheeled it inside their own walls, and at night the soldiers climbed out and opened the gates.
The horse was the vessel that carried the soldiers past the defenses.
And that, Berger says, is exactly what a great story does for your message.
The story is the horse.
Your message is the soldiers hidden inside.
People happily carry the story into their conversations, into their homes, and your message rides along, right past the defenses people normally put up against being sold to.
His central business example is Subway and a man named Jared Fogle.

Jared was a college student who was severely overweight, and he lost an enormous amount of weight, over two hundred pounds, by, among other things, eating Subway sandwiches every day, choosing the lower-fat options.
His story got picked up, and Subway built a whole campaign around it.
And notice what the campaign really did.
It was not a dry ad saying, our sandwiches are lower in fat.
It was a story.
A real young man, unhappy and unhealthy, who transformed his life, and Subway was part of that transformation.
People told each other the Jared story, and buried inside that story was the message Subway wanted to spread, that Subway had seven sandwiches with under six grams of fat.
The health message rode into the culture on the back of a compelling human story.
Nobody sits around telling their friends about grams of fat.
But they will tell the story of the guy who lost half his body weight eating at Subway, and the fat message comes along for the ride.
But Berger gives a critical, critical warning here, and if you take one thing from the Stories principle, take this.
A story is only useful to you if your message is an integral part of the narrative.
The message has to be woven into the story so tightly that you cannot tell the story without telling the message.
Because here is the danger.
You can create a story that everyone loves and shares, and have it do nothing for you, because your brand or your message is just a detail people forget while remembering the fun part.
His cautionary tale is Evian and the roller-skating babies.
Evian, the water company, made a viral ad featuring computer-generated babies dancing and doing elaborate roller-skating stunts, breakdancing, spinning, all these adorable, impossible moves.
It was hugely popular, one of the most viewed online ads of its time, tens of millions of views, shared everywhere.
A massive success by view count.
And yet, and here is the punchline, Evian's sales did not benefit much, and studies found that many people who loved and shared the video could not even remember that it was for Evian.
The roller-skating babies were the story, and they were delightful, but the water, the actual product, the whole point, was not integral to the story at all.
You could swap in any brand and the video would work exactly the same.
The babies were valuable, but irrelevant to Evian.
So all that sharing energy leaked out into the world and carried Evian's message nowhere.
Contrast that with the hundred-dollar cheesesteak we started with.
You literally cannot tell that story without mentioning the restaurant and the outrageous ingredients, because the restaurant and the ingredients are the story.
There is no version of that anecdote that forgets the point.
That is the test.
Ask, if someone tells my story, do they have to mention my product or message to tell it at all?
Or can they tell the whole thing and leave me out?
If they can leave you out, you have built roller-skating babies, and you need to go back and weave your message into the bones of the narrative so that it cannot be extracted.
Make your desired message an integral part of the plot, not a sticker on the outside of it.
So there they are, all six.
Social Currency, we share what makes us look good.
Triggers, top of mind, tip of tongue, we talk about what is activated around us.
Emotion, when we care, we share, and high arousal is the key.
Public, built to show, built to grow.
Practical Value, news you can use, we share to help.
And Stories, information rides inside narratives like soldiers in a Trojan horse.
STEPPS.
Those are the ingredients of contagious content.

And I want to circle back to the biggest, most freeing idea in the whole book before we finish, because it is easy to hear these six principles and think they are tricks for people with big budgets and marketing departments.
They are not.
Berger is adamant on this point.
You do not need a large budget.
You do not need luck.
You do not need to be famous or well-connected or to catch a celebrity's eye.
Not one of these six principles requires money.
A secret bar spends nothing on advertising.
The cheesesteak story spread for free.
The corn-shucking grandpa was a home video.
The Livestrong bracelet is a piece of rubber.
What all of these things have in common is not resources.
It is design.
Someone took the time to build one or more of these six ingredients into the thing itself.
Contagiousness is not a property of the wallet.
It is a property of the product.
It is baked in, not bolted on, and anyone who is willing to think carefully about their message can bake it in.
So let me leave you with something concrete, a set of things you can actually do this week to make your own idea, product, or work more contagious.
Not someday.
This week.
First, run your thing through all six principles as a checklist, honestly.
Sit down, take whatever you are trying to spread, and ask the six questions, one at a time.
Does talking about this make people look good, that is Social Currency.
Is there a trigger, something in daily life that will regularly remind people of it.
Does it make people feel a high-arousal emotion, awe, excitement, amusement, or even anger.
Is it public, can people see when others use it or support it.
Does it have practical value, does it genuinely help someone.
And is it wrapped in a story that carries the message where the message cannot be removed.
Most things you make will hit maybe one of these by accident.
Your job this week is to consciously strengthen at least two or three.
Second, find your trigger.
This is the one people most often overlook, and it is one of the most powerful.
Look at the daily life of the person you want talking about you and find a frequent, recurring cue you can link yourself to.
A time of day, a common object, a routine, a place, a habit like the morning coffee that Kit Kat grabbed onto.
Then start deliberately connecting your message to that cue, over and over, until the cue reactivates your idea on its own, for free, every day.
Third, build in something to show.
Look at your product or cause and ask, when someone uses it or supports it, what is the visible residue?
If there is none, create one.
A badge, a sticker, a distinctive color, a signature line, a physical token, a shareable result, anything that turns a private action into a public signal.
Remember the white earbuds and the glowing Apple and the yellow bracelet.
Make the using of it visible.
Fourth, and this is maybe the most important, fix your story.
If you are relying on a story to carry your message, do the roller-skating-babies test on it right now.
Tell your story out loud, or imagine a friend retelling it, and check, can they tell the whole thing without mentioning your product or your point?
If yes, your message is a bolt-on, and it will fall off in transit.
Rewrite it so the message is load-bearing, so the story collapses without it, the way the cheesesteak story collapses without the restaurant.
Weave the message into the bones.
And fifth, stop worrying about finding the right influential person, and start obsessing over making the message itself worth passing on.
That reframing alone will change how you spend your energy.
Every hour you were about to spend chasing some special messenger, spend it instead making the thing more remarkable, more triggered, more emotional, more public, more useful, more story-shaped.
Because the truth Berger proved is that a contagious message does not need a special carrier.
Ordinary people, your customers, your readers, your friends, will carry a genuinely contagious idea for you, willingly, for free, because in sharing it they are getting something too.
They look good, they help someone, they feel something, they tell a good story.
That is the deep lesson of "Contagious" by Jonah Berger.
Word of mouth beats advertising, and word of mouth is not luck, it is engineering.
It is not about who is talking, it is about whether what you built gave them a reason to.
Six ingredients, Social Currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical Value, and Stories.
Bake even a couple of them right into the heart of your work, and you stop hoping your idea catches on, and you start building an idea that was made to catch on all along.
Now go make something worth talking about.
